facade_colour: tan/beige painted wood siding on upper floor, dark brown/black painted commercial ground floor with yellow trim accents

hcd_data.architectural_style: Converted House-form, Semi-detached (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as an early-twentieth-century vernacular style two-storey commercial addition to a nineteenth-century residential structure. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, the property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed between 1924 and 1930 onto the principal elevation of a pre-1889 residence, the property has retained its brick facade and pair of second storey window openings with segmentally arched brick voussoirs. The property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area. With its visible layering of residential and commercial built forms, the property is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way SB | 30 km/h | Meets minimum standards | Secondary commercial spine

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: tan/beige painted wood clapboard siding on upper floor, dark brown/black commercial ground floor
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Night photo. Upper floor clad in tan/beige painted wood clapboard with 2 windows. Ground floor has large storefront window with red-painted dividing element. Graffiti on adjacent wall.
photo_observations.window_details: Ground floor: storefront glazing. Upper floor: 2 small rectangular windows in clapboard siding.
photo_observations.door_details: commercial entrance, security gated at night
photo_observations.decorative_details: red vertical stripe separating storefronts, yellow accent trim at cornice line
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: gable visible above, pitched roof behind facade
photo_observations.storefront_description: Large plate glass storefront window at ground level with red-painted divider and brown base panel.
photo_observations.signage_observed: FRIENDLY HOUSE sign visible, yellow banners
photo_observations.alterations_visible: entire facade re-clad with horizontal wood siding on upper floor, commercial conversion at ground level, original brick facade completely covered
photo_observations.notes: Converted House-form per HCD typology. Style corrected from Vernacular to Victorian - the underlying house-form with gable roof dates to the Victorian era despite wood clapboard cladding alteration. Commercial ground floor is expected for this typology.

storefront_description: commercial ground floor with large display windows, graffiti/tags on right wall, security gates
notes: nighttime photo; building is a converted house-form with two-storey commercial addition; original Victorian structure hidden behind siding and commercial facade

facade_description: 71 Kensington Ave presents a converted house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its converted house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed in a tan/beige painted wood siding on upper floor, dark brown/black painted commercial ground floor with yellow trim accents palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade is organized as a storefront base with 2 upper-window bays, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include cornice, decorative brick. HCD note: The property has value as an early-twentieth-century vernacular style two-storey commercial addition to a nineteenth-century residential structure. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, the property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed between 1924 and 1930 onto the principal elevation of a pre-1889 residence, the property has retained its brick facade and pair of second storey window openings with segmentally arched brick voussoirs. The property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area. With its visible layering of residential and commercial built forms, the property is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.
